---
title: "Kullback-Leibler Divergence"
subtitle: "Quantifying the exact amount of information lost when an approximation is used instead of the truth."
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
---

## 1. Definitions {#sec-definitions}

::: {.thm-box .thm-definition}
### Definition: KL Divergence (Relative Entropy)

For discrete probability distributions $P$ and $Q$ defined on the same probability space $\mathcal{X}$, the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence from $Q$ to $P$ is defined as:

$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right)
$$

For continuous distributions with probability density functions $p$ and $q$, the sum becomes an integral:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln \left( \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \right) dx
$$

*(Note: The double vertical bars $\|$ emphasize that this is a divergence between two distributions, not a conditional probability. It is read as "The KL divergence of P with respect to Q" or "The KL divergence from Q to P".)*
:::

---

## 2. Intuitions & Remarks {#sec-intuitions}

::: {.thm-box .thm-remark}
### The Intuition of Information Loss

If $P$ represents the "true" underlying distribution of data in nature, and $Q$ represents our mathematical model's approximation of that distribution, then $D_{KL}(P \| Q)$ quantifies the exact amount of information lost when we use $Q$ instead of $P$.

Imagine you are trying to compress data using a codebook. If you know the true distribution $P$, you can build an optimal codebook. If you are forced to use a codebook optimized for $Q$ instead, your compressed files will be slightly larger. The KL divergence is exactly the number of *extra bits* required due to using the wrong codebook.
:::

---

## 3. Formulas & Key Properties {#sec-properties}

::: {.thm-box .thm-theorem}
### Key Properties of KL Divergence

#### 1. Gibbs' Inequality (Non-negativity)
KL Divergence is strictly non-negative. You cannot have "negative" information loss.
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0
$$
Equality $D_{KL}(P \| Q) = 0$ holds **if and only if** $P$ and $Q$ are identical almost everywhere.

#### 2. Lack of Symmetry
KL Divergence is **not** a true mathematical distance metric because it is highly asymmetric:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \| P)
$$
This asymmetry has profound implications in machine learning, dictating whether models exhibit "mode-seeking" or "mean-seeking" behavior.

#### 3. Lack of Triangle Inequality
It does not satisfy the triangle inequality. You cannot compute the divergence $D_{KL}(P \| R)$ simply by bridging through an intermediate distribution $Q$. Thus, we call it a "divergence" rather than a "distance".
:::

---

## 4. Graphs & Visualizations {#sec-visualizations}

Because of the asymmetry, the order of $P$ and $Q$ drastically alters the geometric behavior of the approximation when we use KL Divergence as an optimization objective.

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    subgraph Forward KL / Mean-Seeking
        A[True Bimodal P] -->|Penalty: Q must cover P| B(Broad Unimodal Q)
        B -.->|Covers both peaks, but underestimates heights| C(Mean-Seeking)
    end
    
    subgraph Reverse KL / Mode-Seeking
        D[True Bimodal P] -->|Penalty: Q avoids P=0| E(Narrow Unimodal Q)
        E -.->|Collapses into one valid peak| F(Mode-Seeking)
    end
    
    style A fill:#161b22,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#161b22,stroke:#f87171,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#161b22,stroke:#00ff88,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    
    style D fill:#161b22,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style E fill:#161b22,stroke:#f87171,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#161b22,stroke:#00ff88,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

#### Forward KL: $D_{KL}(P \| Q)$
- **Penalty**: If $P(x) > 0$ but $Q(x) \to 0$, the ratio $\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ explodes to infinity. Therefore, $Q$ is forced to place probability mass *everywhere* $P$ places mass.
- **Result**: $Q$ stretches out to "cover" all the modes (peaks) of $P$, resulting in a broad, **mean-seeking** approximation.

#### Reverse KL: $D_{KL}(Q \| P)$
- **Penalty**: If $Q(x) > 0$ but $P(x) \to 0$, the ratio $\frac{Q(x)}{P(x)}$ explodes. Therefore, $Q$ must absolutely avoid placing mass where $P$ has no mass.
- **Result**: $Q$ tends to "collapse" into a single valid peak (mode) of $P$ to safely avoid areas where $P=0$. This results in a highly focused, **mode-seeking** approximation.

---

## 5. Relations to Other Fields {#sec-relations}

| Field | Interpretation / Mapping | Application Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Variational Inference** | We approximate complex posteriors with simple networks by explicitly minimizing the Reverse KL Divergence. | Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | We add a KL penalty to ensure newly updated policies do not diverge too wildly from old policies. | Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) |
| **Information Bottleneck** | Networks try to maximize mutual information with targets while minimizing KL divergence with inputs. | Deep Learning Theory |

---

## 6. Interactive Simulation {#sec-simulation}

```{=html}
<div class="simulation-card" style="background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.03); border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1); border-radius: 16px; padding: 2rem; margin: 2rem 0; box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(0,0,0,0.4); backdrop-filter: blur(12px);">
  <h4 style="margin-top: 0; color: #fff; font-family: 'Outfit', sans-serif;">Interactive Simulation: The Asymmetry of KL Divergence</h4>
  <p style="color: #a0a2b6; font-size: 0.95rem; margin-bottom: 1.5rem;">
    Let $P$ be a fixed coin with $P(\text{Heads}) = 0.2$. You control the approximation $Q(\text{Heads}) = q$. Drag the slider to adjust $q$ and see how the Forward KL Divergence $D_{KL}(P\|Q)$ and Reverse KL Divergence $D_{KL}(Q\|P)$ respond differently as you approach the boundaries.
  </p>

  <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 1.5rem; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto;">
    <!-- SVG Chart -->
    <svg id="kl-chart" viewBox="0 0 500 250" style="width: 100%; height: auto; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.2); border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.05);">
      <!-- Grid lines -->
      <line x1="50" y1="200" x2="450" y2="200" stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)" stroke-width="1" />
      <line x1="50" y1="30" x2="50" y2="200" stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)" stroke-width="1" />

      <!-- Axis Labels -->
      <text x="250" y="230" fill="#8b949e" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Outfit">Approximation q</text>
      <text x="20" y="115" fill="#8b949e" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 20 115)" font-family="Outfit">Divergence (bits)</text>

      <text x="50" y="215" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">0.0</text>
      <text x="130" y="215" fill="#60a5fa" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">p=0.2</text>
      <text x="250" y="215" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">0.5</text>
      <text x="450" y="215" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">1.0</text>

      <text x="40" y="203" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="end">0</text>
      <text x="40" y="115" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="end">2</text>
      <text x="40" y="34" fill="#6e7681" font-size="10" text-anchor="end">4</text>

      <!-- P vertical line -->
      <line x1="130" y1="30" x2="130" y2="200" stroke="rgba(96, 165, 250, 0.4)" stroke-dasharray="2,2" />

      <!-- Curve paths -->
      <path id="kl-fwd-curve" d="" fill="none" stroke="#f59e0b" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.8" />
      <path id="kl-rev-curve" d="" fill="none" stroke="#8b5cf6" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.8" />

      <!-- Legend -->
      <rect x="330" y="40" width="10" height="10" fill="#f59e0b" />
      <text x="345" y="49" fill="#c9d1d9" font-size="10">Forward D(P||Q)</text>
      <rect x="330" y="55" width="10" height="10" fill="#8b5cf6" />
      <text x="345" y="64" fill="#c9d1d9" font-size="10">Reverse D(Q||P)</text>

      <!-- Current selection markers -->
      <line id="kl-v-guide" x1="0" y1="200" x2="0" y2="0" stroke="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2)" stroke-dasharray="4,4" />
      <circle id="kl-fwd-point" cx="0" cy="0" r="5" fill="#f59e0b" />
      <circle id="kl-rev-point" cx="0" cy="0" r="5" fill="#8b5cf6" />
    </svg>

    <!-- Controls and Readout -->
    <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto; gap: 1.5rem; align-items: center; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.15); padding: 1rem; border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.03);">
      <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0.5rem;">
        <label for="kl-slider" style="color: #c9d1d9; font-size: 0.85rem; font-weight: 600; font-family: 'Outfit';">Set Approx q:</label>
        <input id="kl-slider" type="range" min="0.01" max="0.99" step="0.01" value="0.5" style="width: 100%; accent-color: #f59e0b; cursor: pointer;" />
      </div>
      <div style="text-align: right; font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.9rem; line-height: 1.4; color: #fff;">
        <div>q = <span id="kl-val-q" style="color: #fff; font-weight: 700;">0.500</span></div>
        <div style="color: #f59e0b;">D(P||Q) = <span id="kl-val-fwd" style="font-weight: 700;">0.000</span></div>
        <div style="color: #8b5cf6;">D(Q||P) = <span id="kl-val-rev" style="font-weight: 700;">0.000</span></div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <script>
    document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function() {
      const slider = document.getElementById("kl-slider");
      const valQ = document.getElementById("kl-val-q");
      const valFwd = document.getElementById("kl-val-fwd");
      const valRev = document.getElementById("kl-val-rev");
      
      const pathFwd = document.getElementById("kl-fwd-curve");
      const pathRev = document.getElementById("kl-rev-curve");
      const pointFwd = document.getElementById("kl-fwd-point");
      const pointRev = document.getElementById("kl-rev-point");
      const vGuide = document.getElementById("kl-v-guide");

      const width = 400; // 450 - 50
      const height = 170; // 200 - 30
      const P = 0.2; // Fixed target probability

      function DKL(true_p, approx_q) {
          if (approx_q <= 0 || approx_q >= 1) return 10;
          let d1 = true_p * Math.log2(true_p / approx_q);
          let d2 = (1 - true_p) * Math.log2((1 - true_p) / (1 - approx_q));
          return d1 + d2;
      }

      // Generate SVG path for the curves
      let dFwd = "";
      let dRev = "";
      for (let q = 0.01; q <= 0.99; q += 0.01) {
        const x = 50 + q * width;
        const fwd = DKL(P, q);
        const rev = DKL(q, P);
        const yFwd = 200 - Math.min((fwd / 4.0), 1) * height; // Scale up to 4 bits max
        const yRev = 200 - Math.min((rev / 4.0), 1) * height; 
        
        dFwd += (q === 0.01 ? "M" : "L") + `${x} ${yFwd}`;
        dRev += (q === 0.01 ? "M" : "L") + `${x} ${yRev}`;
      }
      pathFwd.setAttribute("d", dFwd);
      pathRev.setAttribute("d", dRev);

      function update() {
        const q = parseFloat(slider.value);
        const fwd = DKL(P, q);
        const rev = DKL(q, P);

        valQ.textContent = q.toFixed(3);
        valFwd.textContent = fwd.toFixed(3);
        valRev.textContent = rev.toFixed(3);

        const x = 50 + q * width;
        const yFwd = 200 - Math.min((fwd / 4.0), 1) * height;
        const yRev = 200 - Math.min((rev / 4.0), 1) * height;

        pointFwd.setAttribute("cx", x);
        pointFwd.setAttribute("cy", yFwd);
        
        pointRev.setAttribute("cx", x);
        pointRev.setAttribute("cy", yRev);

        vGuide.setAttribute("x1", x);
        vGuide.setAttribute("y1", 200);
        vGuide.setAttribute("x2", x);
        vGuide.setAttribute("y2", Math.min(yFwd, yRev));
      }

      slider.addEventListener("input", update);
      update();
    });
  </script>
</div>
```

---

## 7. Practice Problems {#sec-problems}

### Problem 1: Proof of Asymmetry {#problem-1 .thm-box .thm-problem}

Provide a concrete, numerical proof that $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \| P)$ using two simple discrete probability distributions: $P=[0.5, 0.5]$ and $Q=[0.9, 0.1]$.

<details class="solution-toggle">
<summary>Reveal Solution to Problem 1</summary>
<div class="solution-body">

::: {.thm-box .thm-solution}
<div class="solution-heading">Solution</div>

Let $P = [0.5, 0.5]$ and $Q = [0.9, 0.1]$.

**Forward KL Divergence:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{KL}(P \| Q) &= \sum P(x) \log_2 \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right) \\
&= 0.5 \log_2\left(\frac{0.5}{0.9}\right) + 0.5 \log_2\left(\frac{0.5}{0.1}\right) \\
&= 0.5 \log_2(0.555) + 0.5 \log_2(5) \\
&\approx 0.5(-0.849) + 0.5(2.321) \\
&\approx -0.424 + 1.161 \\
&= 0.737 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reverse KL Divergence:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{KL}(Q \| P) &= \sum Q(x) \log_2 \left( \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} \right) \\
&= 0.9 \log_2\left(\frac{0.9}{0.5}\right) + 0.1 \log_2\left(\frac{0.1}{0.5}\right) \\
&= 0.9 \log_2(1.8) + 0.1 \log_2(0.2) \\
&\approx 0.9(0.848) + 0.1(-2.321) \\
&\approx 0.763 - 0.232 \\
&= 0.531 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.737 \neq 0.531$, we have proven that the KL divergence is fundamentally asymmetric. $\blacksquare$
:::

</div>
</details>

### Problem 2: Gibbs' Inequality {#problem-2 .thm-box .thm-problem}

Use Jensen's Inequality to mathematically prove Gibbs' Inequality: $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0$.
*(Hint: Remember that Jensen's Inequality states that for any strictly concave function like $\ln(x)$, the expected value of the function is less than or equal to the function of the expected value: $\mathbb{E}[\ln(X)] \le \ln(\mathbb{E}[X])$).*

<details class="solution-toggle">
<summary>Reveal Solution to Problem 2</summary>
<div class="solution-body">

::: {.thm-box .thm-solution}
<div class="solution-heading">Solution</div>

Let's begin with the definition of KL Divergence using the natural logarithm:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{x} P(x) \ln \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right)
$$

We can rewrite the ratio inside the logarithm by negating it:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \ln \left( \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} \right)
$$

Notice that $\sum_{x} P(x) [\dots]$ is exactly the definition of the mathematical expectation $\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P}[\dots]$. Thus:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = -\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right) \right]
$$

Since the natural logarithm function $\ln(z)$ is strictly concave, we can apply Jensen's Inequality, which states that $-\mathbb{E}[\ln(Z)] \ge -\ln(\mathbb{E}[Z])$:
$$
-\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right) \right] \ge -\ln \left( \mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right] \right)
$$

Now, let's evaluate the inner expectation:
$$
\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right] = \sum_{x} P(x) \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} = \sum_{x} Q(x) = 1
$$
Because $Q$ is a valid probability distribution, its probabilities must sum to 1.

Substituting this back into the inequality:
$$
D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge -\ln(1) = 0
$$

Therefore, $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0$. $\blacksquare$
:::

</div>
</details>
